title: [InstCombine] Missed optimization for trunc (Pow2 >> X) to i1
summary:
The original issue was a very specific bit-test hidden as `trunc i8 (lshr i8 4, %x) to i1`. Since `4` is a power of two, shifting it right by `%x` leaves bit 0 set iff `%x == log2(4) == 2`. So the motivating case is really an equality/range test on the shift amount, not a meaningful shift result.

The patch generalizes that into a `visitTrunc()` fold for `trunc (...shr C, V) to i1`, where the source is either `lshr` or `ashr` (`m_Shr` matches both). It recognizes two constant classes:
- `C` is a power of 2:  
  `trunc (shr C, V) to i1  -->  icmp eq V, log2(C)`
- `C` is a low-bit mask (`2^n - 1`):  
  `trunc (shr C, V) to i1  -->  icmp ult V, n`

That captures both “exact one shift amount produces low bit 1” and “any shift below a threshold preserves low bit 1”. The implementation uses `m_Power2`, `m_LowBitMask`, and derives the compare RHS via `countr_zero()` / `countr_one()`.

The dev tests broaden coverage beyond the single motivating case by checking:
- both `lshr` and `ashr`;
- both constant families: `2^n` and `2^n-1`;
- positive and negative examples (`9` should not fold);
- multi-use shift results, ensuring only the `trunc` is rewritten;
- interaction with larger expressions/users (`test1`, `test5`) so downstream simplification still works.

This is a good template: identify a hidden bit predicate, replace it with a direct compare, then test operator variants, constant classes, non-matches, and multi-use safety.
